name: Symmetry Principles
summary: If a physical system looks the same after a transformation (rotation, translation, time shift), there is a corresponding conserved quantity (Noether's theorem).
whenToUseIt: Use symmetry arguments to simplify problems, identify conserved quantities, and constrain the form of physical laws without doing detailed calculations.
steps: Identify any symmetries of the system (rotational, translational, time, mirror, gauge)
Apply Noether's theorem: each continuous symmetry implies a conservation law
Use the symmetry to constrain the solution — e.g. if the system has rotational symmetry, the answer cannot depend on angle
Simplify the problem by choosing coordinates aligned with the symmetry
Verify the result respects all identified symmetries
example: A uniform sphere has rotational symmetry. Therefore its gravitational field can only depend on distance from the centre, not on direction. This insight lets you treat the sphere as a point mass for objects outside it — Newton's shell theorem — without computing any integrals.
